definition: A curse is a malediction — words or rites meant to bring harm, misfortune, or doom on a target. It is the oldest hostile magic, and among the best-attested in the archaeological record.
history: *Curse* is an etymological dead end: Old English *curs*, of genuinely unknown origin, with no secure cognate in any related language — a curse-word whose own past is, fittingly, obscure. The practice is ancient and vivid: Greek and Roman *defixiones*, lead tablets inscribed with binding curses, survive by the thousand. Magic makes 'Curse' an Aura subtype — an enchantment you fix onto an opponent.

def_sources.label: John Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells

commentary: The curse-tablet is the classic battlefield for the 'magic versus religion' problem in antiquity. An older Frazerian view sorted the coercive *defixio* (magic) from the supplicant prayer (religion) as a difference of kind. H. S. Versnel undid the tidy split, showing 'prayers for justice' that beg the gods exactly as cursers command the dead, and John Gager's sourcebook demonstrates how thoroughly the *defixiones* borrow liturgical form. Recent work by Jessica Lamont and Stuart McKie pushes further, reading the tablets as ordinary social action rather than a deviant category — so that 'magic' becomes a label historians must justify, not assume.

commentary_sources.label: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World
Jessica L. Lamont, In Blood and Ashes
Stuart McKie, Living and Cursing in the Roman West

etymology.origin: Old English curs — origin genuinely unknown
etymology.note: One of the rare English words with no established cognate in any related language; the etymology is a real dead-end, which is itself eerie.
etymology.hook: A curse-word whose own origins are cursed to obscurity.

essay.title: Curse: a word whose own origins are cursed
essay.dek: An etymological dead end — fitting for a word about doom.
essay.html:
Almost every word in this study can be traced somewhere — to Greek, to Latin, to Arabic, to a Persian priesthood. Curse cannot. Old English curs appears before 1050 with no secure cognate in any related language: not in the other Germanic tongues, not in Latin, not anywhere. It is one of the genuine dead ends of English etymology — a curse-word whose own past is, fittingly, cursed to obscurity.

The obscurity of the word is in inverse proportion to the vividness of the thing. The curse is the best-attested magic in the entire archaeological record. From across the Greek and Roman world survive thousands of defixiones — thin lead tablets inscribed with binding curses, folded, pierced with a nail, and buried in graves or wells or springs. John Gager's collection catalogues them: curses against rival shopkeepers, against chariot teams, against lovers and thieves and litigants. This is not the rarefied magic of the philosopher-magus; it is the everyday hostility of ordinary people, made permanent in lead.

Magic: The Gathering makes 'Curse' an Aura subtype — an enchantment you fasten onto an opponent, so it sits on their side of the table, doing them slow harm. That is, remarkably, the exact logic of the defixio: a small inscribed object, aimed at a specific person, that binds misfortune to them at a distance. The oldest hostile magic we can dig up is still, on the cards, hostile magic — and the word for it still refuses to say where it came from.

sources.label: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World

flavor_snippets.html: Snake-Eyes's gift takes others back to their worst memory. To the weak, it is a <mark>curse</mark>; to the wise, a second chance.
Odric resisted the vampire's <mark>curse</mark> with all his strength. But after a lifetime of fighting monsters, he was doomed to become one himself.
The dead who wander beyond the safety of the city crave only to spread their <mark>curse</mark>.

"<mark>Curse</mark> Reveka, and <mark>curse</mark> her coddled conjurers. Their sorcerers' school shall yet be ours."
—Irini Sengir
Crovax was destined for an angel's <mark>curse</mark>, but one warped timeline saw the noble redeemed.
A haunted husk of bark, its <mark>cursed</mark> whispers linger on the wind.
The priests tried <mark>cursing</mark> it. The mages tried dispelling it. In the end, they all obeyed it.
"Begone, moon-<mark>cursed</mark>! These folk are no prey of yours."
Humans still feared wolves, though no connection between them and the <mark>curse</mark> of lycanthropy had ever been proved.

"Those ogres who did not embrace oni worship were cast out, <mark>cursed</mark>, and forced into hermitage, waiting for the day the oni would come for them."
—*The History of Kamigawa*
The <mark>curse</mark> of Redtooth Keep could only be broken by a mystical rose that blooms in moonlight.

"A <mark>cursed</mark> silhouette to look upon. Even if the halls stand empty, its vile shadows blight the land. I shudder to think of the evils that it held."
—Koth

"May you wake to find you were only ever a dream."
—Mephidross <mark>curse</mark>

mechanics_snippets.html: For each creature your opponents control, create a <mark>Cursed</mark> Role token attached to that creature. (If you control another Role on it, put that one into the graveyard. Enchanted creature is 1/1.)

Lifelink
When this creature enters, create a <mark>Cursed</mark> Role token attached to it. (Enchanted creature is 1/1.)
I — Create a <mark>Cursed</mark> Role token attached to up to one target creature. (If you control another Role on it, put that one into the graveyard. Enchanted creature is 1/1.)
Baki's <mark>Curse</mark> deals 2 damage to each creature for each Aura attached to that creature.
As this permanent transforms into <mark>Curse</mark> of Leeches, attach it to a player.

Trample
Lizard Formula — When Lizard, Connors's <mark>Curse</mark> enters, up to one other target creature loses all abilities and becomes a green Lizard creature with base power and toughness 4/4.

Whenever Celestine Cave Witch attacks, you may sacrifice an Insect. When you do, <mark>curse</mark> defending player. (Create a black Aura <mark>Curse</mark> enchantment token that's attached to that player. It has enchant player and "At the beginning of your upkeep, you lose 1 life.")

Enchant player
At the beginning of enchanted player's upkeep, any number of target players other than that player each draw cards equal to the number of <mark>Curses</mark> attached to that player.
